file_name: Meghatria et al. - 2025 - Enhancing skin cancer detection using curriculum learning in ensemble deep learning context.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically ensemble deep learning with curriculum learning, to classify skin lesions (melanoma vs. nevus). It employs Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) such as VGG16, ResNet50, and EfficientNetB0 on the ISIC 2019 and 2020 dermoscopic image datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results, specifically investigating curriculum learning in an ensemble deep learning context for melanoma detection. It details a rigorous experimental study on ISIC 2019 and 2020 datasets, comparing transfer learning, bagging, and curriculum learning strategies using CNNs (VGG16, ResNet50, EfficientNetB0), and reports new performance metrics (e.g., F1-score of 90.77%). Although it includes a related work section, its primary purpose is reporting new empirical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Enhancing Skin Cancer Detection Using Curriculum Learning In Ensemble Deep Learning Context
journal: ProfIT AI’25: 5th International Workshop of IT-professionals on Artificial Intelligence
brief_summary: This study investigates the use of curriculum learning within an ensemble deep learning context to enhance the binary classification of melanoma versus nevus. The researchers evaluated five pre-trained CNN architectures (VGG16, ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, InceptionV3, and Xception) using transfer learning, bagging ensembles, and a novel curriculum learning strategy on a combined dataset derived from ISIC 2019 and 2020 challenges. The curriculum learning approach progressively trained the EfficientNetB0 model on data ordered by diagnostic difficulty, aiming to improve robustness and accuracy. Experimental results demonstrated that this method significantly outperformed conventional fine-tuning and standard ensemble bagging techniques. Specifically, the curriculum learning model achieved a superior F1-score of 90.77%, highlighting its potential for improving automated melanoma diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task. Derived from ISIC 2019 (4,522 cases) and ISIC 2020 (584 cases) datasets, totaling 5,106 images in the study's combined dataset.
One of the two classes in the binary classification task (benign comparator to melanoma). Totaling 6,343 images in the study's combined dataset.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma versus nevus categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'binary classification of melanoma versus nevus' using dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2019 and 2020 datasets. The models predict discrete diagnostic labels (melanoma or nevus) using CNN architectures (VGG16, ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, etc.) with transfer learning, bagging, and curriculum learning strategies. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019, 2020
datasets_used.description: A fusion of the ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 challenges dermoscopic image datasets. The study selected only two diagnostic categories (melanoma and nevus) from these sources, resulting in a total of 11,449 images. To mitigate class imbalance, random under-sampling was applied to the majority class.

use.training: Used for training CNN models (VGG16, ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, etc.) via transfer learning, bagging ensembles, and curriculum learning strategies. The curriculum learning approach specifically partitioned the training set into five difficulty levels based on classifier confidence.
use.validation: Used for periodic performance assessment and refinement of model parameters during the curriculum learning training phase.
use.testing: Used as an independent, unseen test set for rigorous assessment of robustness and diagnostic accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score) without statistical significance testing.

Transfer Learning - Feature Extraction (.notes: ResNet50 and EfficientNetB0 achieved the highest F1-scores among the models in this scenario.

Transfer Learning - Fine-Tuning (Scenari.notes: VGG16 achieved the highest performance with an F1-score of 89%.

Ensemble Learning - Bagging (EfficientNe.notes: The ensemble aggregated predictions from multiple independently trained instances of EfficientNetB0 models.

Curriculum Learning (EfficientNetB0).notes: This approach yielded the most significant performance gains, surpassing both single-model transfer learning and ensemble bagging approaches. The curriculum learning involved structuring the training process to present images in increasing order of complexity across five difficulty levels.

architecture_used.main_model: EfficientNetB0 (primary focus for curriculum learning and ensemble bagging); VGG16, ResNet50, InceptionV3, and Xception (evaluated in transfer learning scenarios)
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224x64 (as depicted in Figure 2 and Figure 3 for VGG16 example; specific input size for other models not explicitly stated but implied to be standard for these architectures)

training_modes: Fine-Tuning (partial unfreezing of upper convolutional layers)
architecture_details.ensemble_method: Bagging (bootstrap aggregating) using multiple instances of EfficientNetB0 trained on distinct bootstrap-resampled subsets
architecture_details.curriculum_learning_strategy: Difficulty-based ranking using classifier confidence scores (softmax activation), partitioning data into 5 difficulty levels (Very certain to Highly uncertain), progressive training from simple to complex samples, followed by consolidation on the full dataset

training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Random under-sampling of the majority class (Nevus)

preprocessing_techniques: Random under-sampling of the majority class (Nevus) to mitigate class imbalance
Data augmentation including random flips, brightness/contrast/saturation adjustments, and random crops
Difficulty-based ranking of samples using classifier confidence scores (softmax activation) for curriculum learning
Partitioning of training data into five difficulty levels based on confidence ranges

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to enhance generalization and mitigate overfitting.
Applied during training to enhance generalization and mitigate overfitting.
Applied during training to enhance generalization and mitigate overfitting.

conclusions: Curriculum learning applied to the EfficientNetB0 architecture significantly enhances binary classification of melanoma versus nevus, achieving a superior F1-score of 90.77%.
The proposed curriculum learning strategy outperforms conventional transfer learning (both feature extraction and fine-tuning modes) and standard ensemble bagging approaches.

Among the evaluated pre-trained CNN architectures (VGG16, ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, InceptionV3, and Xception), EfficientNetB0 and ResNet50 demonstrated superior capability in capturing discriminative features for dermoscopic images.

Integrating curriculum learning into an ensemble deep learning context improves the robustness and accuracy of automated melanoma diagnosis by progressively guiding the model from simple to complex samples based on diagnostic difficulty.

The study validates the effectiveness of confidence-based difficulty ranking and progressive training on combined ISIC 2019 and 2020 datasets for addressing challenges such as class imbalance and intra-class variability.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (melanoma vs. nevus) derived from ISIC 2019 and 2020 datasets, excluding other skin cancer types and clinical contexts.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons rely solely on descriptive metrics.

The curriculum learning strategy relies on classifier confidence scores to determine sample difficulty, which introduces a potential circularity or data leakage risk if the confidence estimates are not robustly decoupled from the final evaluation metrics.

The dataset was artificially balanced using random under-sampling of the majority class (nevus), which may not reflect the true prevalence distribution in clinical practice and could affect generalizability.
The paper lacks external validation on independent, out-of-distribution datasets, limiting the assessment of real-world clinical robustness and generalizability.
The work was published in a workshop proceedings (ProfIT AI’25), which may indicate a shorter review cycle or lower barrier to entry compared to major peer-reviewed journals.
The authors explicitly state that no Generative AI tools were employed, ensuring the integrity of the generated text and code.
The proposed method increases computational complexity due to the ensemble nature of the curriculum learning approach, which may hinder deployment in resource-constrained clinical settings.

dataset_usage_details.source_pdf_path: /home/agarciag/Desktop/skin_cancer_review/llm_agent/pdfs/compliant/processed_Meghatria et al. - 2025 - Enhancing skin cancer detection using curriculum learning in ensemble deep learning context.pdf
dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized a combined dataset derived from the ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 challenges, specifically selecting the melanoma (MEL) and nevus (NV) classes. The total dataset consisted of 11,449 images (5,106 MEL, 6,343 NV). To address class imbalance, random under-sampling was applied to the majority class (NV). The dataset was split into training, validation, and test sets using stratified splits. The models were pre-trained on ImageNet. The curriculum learning approach involved partitioning the training set into five difficulty levels based on classifier confidence scores derived from an initial pass or preliminary model. No external or cross-dataset validation was performed; all experiments were conducted on the internal splits of the combined ISIC dataset.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper does not report any cross-dataset validation. All models were trained and tested on subsets of the combined ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 dataset. The authors explicitly note in the limitations that the study lacks external validation on independent, out-of-distribution datasets.

training.dataset: ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 (Combined, balanced via under-sampling)
training.counts: Approximately 7,633 images (estimated as 2/3 of 11,449, though exact split ratio not explicitly stated, stratified split mentioned)
training.notes: Used for training CNN models (VGG16, ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, InceptionV3, Xception) via transfer learning, bagging ensembles, and curriculum learning. For curriculum learning, this set was partitioned into five difficulty levels based on confidence scores.

validation.dataset: ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 (Combined, balanced via under-sampling)
validation.counts: Approximately 1,912 images (estimated as 1/6 of 11,449, though exact split ratio not explicitly stated, stratified split mentioned)
validation.notes: Used for periodic performance assessment and refinement of model parameters during the curriculum learning training phase and for model selection/tuning in other scenarios.

testing.dataset: ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 (Combined, balanced via under-sampling)
testing.counts: Approximately 1,912 images (estimated as 1/6 of 11,449, though exact split ratio not explicitly stated, stratified split mentioned)
testing.notes: Used as an independent, unseen test set for final performance evaluation. The paper refers to this as an 'unseen dataset' in the context of the training/validation split, but it is drawn from the same source distribution.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: Used to initialize weights for the five CNN architectures (VGG16, ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, InceptionV3, Xception) before fine-tuning or feature extraction on the dermatological dataset.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Data augmentation techniques including random flips, brightness/contrast/saturation adjustments, and random crops were applied to the training data to enhance generalization and mitigate overfitting. Random under-sampling was used to balance classes.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: Stratified train/validation/test splits were used. Exact percentages or fold counts are not explicitly reported in the text, but the total dataset size is 11,449 images. The curriculum learning method specifically partitions the training set into 5 difficulty levels.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No external unseen dataset was used. The 'unseen' test set refers to the held-out portion of the combined ISIC 2019/2020 dataset not used for training or validation.

dataset_level_results.notes: Results for InceptionV3, Xception, VGG16, EfficientNetB0, and ResNet50. Best F1-score was 88.79% (ResNet50).
Results for InceptionV3, EfficientNetB0, ResNet50, Xception, and VGG16. Best F1-score was 89% (VGG16).

The paper explicitly states that it lacks external validation on independent, out-of-distribution datasets, limiting the assessment of real-world clinical robustness and generalizability.
The dataset was artificially balanced using random under-sampling of the majority class (nevus), which may not reflect the true prevalence distribution in clinical practice.

The curriculum learning strategy relies on classifier confidence scores to determine sample difficulty, which introduces a potential circularity or data leakage risk if the confidence estimates are not robustly decoupled from the final evaluation metrics.
